sourceMovie.title: Who Framed Roger Rabbit
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988) stuck because it commits to its fantasy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Robert Zemeckis's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The fantasy and animation blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Scooby-Doo! Abracadabra-Doo
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Who Framed Roger Rabbit's fantasy stayed with you, Scooby-Doo! Abracadabra-Doo (2010) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The animation through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Zootopia
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Who Framed Roger Rabbit excels in animation, Zootopia (2016) doubles down on comedy to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: The Big Lebowski
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Big Lebowski (1998) is the natural next step after Who Framed Roger Rabbit: same comedy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and crime elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: O Brother, Where Art Thou?
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Who Framed Roger Rabbit who respond to its comedy will find O Brother, Where Art Thou? (2000) immediately familiar. Both films share a crime sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Some Like It Hot
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Who Framed Roger Rabbit fans keep landing on Some Like It Hot (1959) — the comedy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Zootopia 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Zootopia 2 draws from the same animation well as Who Framed Roger Rabbit, channeling comedy in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2025 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Looney Tunes: Back in Action
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Looney Tunes: Back in Action (2003) captures the animation appeal of Who Framed Roger Rabbit without simply imitating it. The comedy streak running through both films is what puts Looney Tunes: Back in Action at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Turner & Hooch
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The comedy appeal of Who Framed Roger Rabbit — that funny edge, the crime — translates cleanly into Turner & Hooch (1989). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Chip 'n Dale: Rescue Rangers
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Chip 'n Dale: Rescue Rangers (2022) clearly understood what makes animation work — the same quality that keeps Who Framed Roger Rabbit rewatchable. Expect the same funny rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Thursday Murder Club
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Thursday Murder Club (2025) is the kind of film Who Framed Roger Rabbit fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The comedy overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after Who Framed Roger Rabbit?
faq.answer: Try Scooby-Doo! Abracadabra-Doo, Zootopia, The Big Lebowski—each shares DNA with Who Framed Roger Rabbit while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Who Framed Roger Rabbit?
faq.answer: Who Framed Roger Rabbit (1988) is best described as Fantasy, Animation, Comedy, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Who Framed Roger Rabbit worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this fantasy lane.
